title: Witch hazel and rose water toner
subtitle: A simple astringent toner for normal to oily skin
excerpt: A three-ingredient toner combining witch hazel distillate, rose water, and aloe vera juice. Witch hazel tightens pores; rose water calms and lightly conditions; aloe vera soothes. Suitable for normal to oily skin after cleansing.
sourceNotes: Witch hazel distillate astringent action: standard pharmacopoeia references (British Pharmacopoeia, Hamamelis virginiana entry). Rose water cosmetic use: Marise Schoenmakers and others, Cosmetic Science and Technology (2017). Aloe vera juice as cosmetic ingredient: International Journal of Molecular Sciences, aloe vera review (2015, open access).

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 spray or dropper bottle (~100 ml, approximately 30 to 50 daily applications)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. This is a water-containing formula; use within 2 weeks without a preservative, or add a broad-spectrum preservative for 3-month shelf life.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make fresh, use within 2 weeks, or add preservative as noted.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Without a preservative, this toner keeps 2 weeks in the fridge. Discard if it becomes cloudy, develops an off smell, or shows any discolouration. To extend shelf life to 3 months, add broad-spectrum preservative at the rate specified on the product data sheet (typically 0.5 to 1% of total formula weight).

glossaryTerms.definition: Apply a small amount to the inside of the forearm and leave for 24 to 48 hours. Witch hazel is generally well-tolerated but can cause dryness in sensitive skin at high concentrations.
A preservative effective against both bacteria and fungi. Required in any water-containing cosmetic for a useful shelf life. Common options include Optiphen, Geogard ECT, and Liquid Germall Plus.

content.text: This toner is three ingredients: witch hazel distillate, rose water, and aloe vera juice. Witch hazel distillate is a mild astringent that tightens pores and removes residual surface oil after cleansing. Rose water contributes a soft floral scent and a slight conditioning quality. Aloe vera juice adds a calming note suitable for skin prone to redness. The ratio given here is 50% witch hazel, 40% rose water, and 10% aloe vera juice; it can be adjusted to suit skin type. Reducing the witch hazel to 30% and increasing the rose water suits drier skin. The formula contains water, so without a

it keeps only 2 weeks in the fridge. Adding a preservative at the standard rate extends the shelf life to 3 months. This is a straightforward formula, suitable as a first water-phase cosmetic project.

items.prepNote: 50% of formula; distillate, not extract (alcohol content 14% in the distillate is standard)
40% of formula; cosmetic grade rose water
10% of formula; inner leaf juice, not gel
0.5% of formula by weight; add if making ahead; follow product data sheet dosage

content.text: Combine witch hazel distillate, rose water, and aloe vera juice in a clean spray bottle. If adding a preservative, dissolve it in the witch hazel first before combining with the other ingredients. Fit the spray cap and shake once to combine. Label the bottle with the contents and the date made.

on the inside of the forearm before applying to the face. Witch hazel distillate is well-tolerated by most skin types but can cause dryness in sensitive or reactive skin; if dryness occurs, reduce the witch hazel proportion to 30%.

Apply to clean skin after washing the face. Spray 2 to 3 times onto the face and neck, or apply with a reusable cotton round. Allow to dry before applying moisturiser or serum. Use morning and evening, or morning only if skin tends toward dryness.

Keep in the fridge. Without a preservative, use within 2 weeks; discard if cloudy or if an off smell develops. With a broad-spectrum preservative added at the standard rate, shelf life extends to 3 months at room temperature.
